label: Faithful Companion
dramatic_function: Embodies the values the protagonist has forgotten or abandoned. Does not solve the problem — holds the protagonist accountable to who they said they were.
relationship_to_protagonist: Unconditional loyalty that becomes a burden when the protagonist starts compromising their core values.
primary_fear: Being abandoned by the one person they gave everything to protect.
worldview_logic: Loyalty is the only currency that cannot be counterfeited. I stay when leaving would be easier — that is the entire point of me.
philosophical_anchor: What is the difference between loyalty and complicity when the person you protect is wrong?
film_examples: Sam Gamgee — The Lord of the Rings ($2.9B total)

label: Mirror Self
dramatic_function: Shows the protagonist exactly who they will become if they stay on their current path. May not know they are a warning. The audience sees the destination before the protagonist does.
relationship_to_protagonist: Parallel version of the protagonist who made a different choice at the same fork — one step ahead on the wrong road.
primary_fear: That the protagonist will see through them and realise the path leads nowhere.
worldview_logic: I am the protagonist plus one more step down the wrong road. I do not see myself as a warning — I see myself as proof that the path was worth taking.
philosophical_anchor: How much of who we are is genuine choice versus the accumulated momentum of earlier choices?
film_examples: Gollum — The Lord of the Rings (dual protagonist/warning)

label: Shadow Teacher
dramatic_function: Possesses the knowledge the protagonist needs but will only pass it on at a cost the protagonist does not understand yet. The lesson always contains a trap.
relationship_to_protagonist: Asymmetric: the teacher knows what the learning will cost; the student does not. The teaching is genuine; the concealment is self-protective.
primary_fear: Being replaced or made irrelevant once the student surpasses them.
worldview_logic: The teaching is real. What I conceal is the cost — because if they knew, they would refuse the lesson, and the lesson is necessary.
philosophical_anchor: Is it ethical to teach what is true if you know the truth will damage the student?
film_examples: Yoda — The Empire Strikes Back ($538M on $18M)

label: Rival Turned Ally
dramatic_function: Starts as the protagonist's greatest obstacle. The reversal — when they join forces — is the story's structural midpoint. Their cooperation is unstable; old resentment resurfaces at the worst moment.
relationship_to_protagonist: Competitive equality. Neither can succeed without the other, and both know it, and both hate it.
primary_fear: Having to admit they were wrong about the protagonist — which means admitting they were wrong about themselves.
worldview_logic: Competition was the only way I knew how to measure myself. Cooperating means admitting the measure was wrong — and I built my entire identity on it.
philosophical_anchor: Can former enemies ever truly collaborate, or does the original conflict always live inside the alliance?
film_examples: Zuko — Avatar: The Last Airbender ($10M+ merchandising)
Draco Malfoy (late arc) — Harry Potter ($7.7B total)

label: Trickster Ally
dramatic_function: Refuses to follow the rules the protagonist believes must be followed. Their irreverence exposes the arbitrariness of the system. Does not solve problems — dissolves the frameworks that made the problems seem unsolvable.
relationship_to_protagonist: Disruptive affection. They like the protagonist precisely because the protagonist is too serious, and they know the mission needs someone who can laugh at it.
primary_fear: Being taken seriously — which would end the freedom that makes them effective.
worldview_logic: Every system is a story someone is telling you about reality. I just refuse to believe the story. My irreverence is not apathy — it is clarity.
philosophical_anchor: Is irreverence a form of genuine clarity, or a sophisticated form of escape from consequence?
film_examples: Han Solo — Star Wars ($775M original trilogy)
Rocket Raccoon — Guardians of the Galaxy ($773M)

label: Wounded Healer
dramatic_function: Carries a wound that is a more advanced version of the protagonist's wound. Heals others precisely because they cannot heal themselves. Their recovery arc mirrors and drives the protagonist's.
relationship_to_protagonist: Parallel journey at a different stage. The protagonist's healing is the wounded healer's vicarious redemption.
primary_fear: That healing the protagonist will prove they are incapable of healing themselves.
worldview_logic: My wound is the credential. I can reach the protagonist because I have been where they are and did not look away — even when I should have.
philosophical_anchor: Can you only heal others after being broken yourself, or does the wound eventually become an excuse?
film_examples: Sean Maguire — Good Will Hunting ($225M on $9M)

label: Innocent Witness
dramatic_function: Sees the world without the protagonist's conditioning or compromise. Their naivety is not a weakness — it is a diagnostic instrument. When they are disturbed by something the protagonist has normalised, the audience sees the truth.
relationship_to_protagonist: Protective instinct in the protagonist — which becomes a mirror when the protagonist realises they are the danger.
primary_fear: That the world is exactly as dark as the adults around them behave — and that their innocence is already lost.
worldview_logic: I see what I see. I do not understand why everyone else pretends not to. The emperor has no clothes — I am the only one who has not been taught the social cost of saying so.
philosophical_anchor: Is innocence a form of truth-telling that adults trade away for comfort, and is the trade ever worth making?
film_examples: Ellie — Up (Pixar's emotional anchor for $731M film)
Bruno — The Boy in the Striped Pyjamas

label: Lost Soul
dramatic_function: Identical starting conditions to the protagonist — same wound, same world, same opportunity — but made the wrong choice at the key moment. Their fate is the road not taken. Their destruction is the cost the protagonist avoids, or doesn't.
relationship_to_protagonist: Tragic symmetry. The protagonist cannot save them because saving them would require admitting the protagonist nearly went the same way.
primary_fear: Being forgiven — because forgiveness would mean the choices that destroyed them were not their fault.
worldview_logic: At some point I chose the easier version of myself. I don't need the protagonist to save me — I need them to not make the same choice. That is the only dignity I have left.
philosophical_anchor: Is a person's fate sealed by a single choice or by the pattern of all their choices — and does the answer change their culpability?
film_examples: Jaime Lannister arc — Game of Thrones
Charlie — The Perks of Being a Wallflower

label: Reluctant Expert
dramatic_function: Has exactly the knowledge or capability the protagonist needs, and wants nothing to do with the mission. Their resistance is not cowardice — it is hard-won wisdom about what this kind of mission costs. They join because the protagonist shows them something they thought was gone.
relationship_to_protagonist: Respect earned rather than given. Their engagement is conditional and can be withdrawn — which creates perpetual tension.
primary_fear: Being pulled back into a world they escaped at great personal cost.
worldview_logic: I know exactly what this kind of mission costs. I am here because the protagonist showed me something I thought was gone: a reason the cost is worth paying one more time.
philosophical_anchor: What is the relationship between expertise and wisdom — can you have one without the other, and which is more dangerous alone?
film_examples: Mr. Miyagi — The Karate Kid ($90M on $8M)

label: Voice of Civilization
dramatic_function: Represents what is worth protecting — the ordinary life, the institution, the community — in contrast to the protagonist's extraordinary mission. Their presence forces the protagonist to answer: what exactly are you risking all this for?
relationship_to_protagonist: Moral ballast. They do not go on the journey, but their existence defines the stakes of the journey.
primary_fear: That the protagonist will succeed at the mission but lose the thing the mission was supposed to protect.
worldview_logic: I am everything the mission is supposed to protect. My ordinariness is the point — the hero is only a hero if the ordinary world is worth returning to.
philosophical_anchor: What makes ordinary life worth protecting, and does the act of protecting it destroy the ordinariness that made it worth protecting?
film_examples: Moneypenny / M — James Bond ($6.8B total franchise)
